notes: MITRE operates several frameworks for describing adversary tactics, techniques, and procedures (TTPs). ATT&CK is the industry-standard cyber adversary behavior framework. ATLAS extends ATT&CK specifically for AI/ML threats. CAPEC provides higher-abstraction attack patterns that link to CWE weaknesses. Each framework serves different use cases while maintaining structural consistency.

alternate_names: Adversarial Tactics, Techniques, and Common Knowledge
data.description: The industry-standard framework for describing cyber adversary behavior.
data.notes:
ATT&CK documents adversary tactics, techniques, and procedures observed in real-world attacks. Organized into three matrices: Enterprise (Windows, Linux, macOS, Cloud, etc.), Mobile (Android, iOS), and ICS (Industrial Control Systems). Updated quarterly.

ID types: techniques (T-prefixed), sub-techniques (T-prefixed with .NNN suffix), tactics (TA-prefixed), mitigations (M-prefixed), groups (G-prefixed), and software (S-prefixed). Each type has its own URL path.

Sub-technique URL transformation: the dot separator in sub-technique IDs (e.g., T1059.003) becomes a slash in the URL path (e.g., /techniques/T1059/003/). This is the only case where the URL differs from the ID format.

ATT&CK IDs are stable — deprecated techniques are marked but not removed. Maps to mitigations and detection analytics (CAR).

urls.note: STIX 2.1 format data for all ATT&CK matrices

children.description: ATT&CK technique (e.g., T1059 Command and Scripting Interpreter)
ATT&CK sub-technique (e.g., T1059.003 Windows Command Shell). Dot in ID becomes slash in URL.

examples.note: Dot in ID becomes slash in URL path

children.description: ATT&CK mitigation (e.g., M1036 Account Use Policies)

alternate_names: Adversarial Threat Landscape for AI Systems
data.description: ATT&CK-style framework specifically for AI/ML threats.
data.notes:
ATLAS documents adversary tactics and techniques targeting AI/ML systems. Modeled after ATT&CK structure but focused on AI-specific threats: model evasion, data poisoning, model theft, prompt injection, etc. Includes case studies of real-world AI attacks.

All ATLAS IDs use the AML. prefix to distinguish from ATT&CK IDs. Dots in ATLAS IDs are preserved in URLs (unlike ATT&CK sub-techniques). Technique sub-techniques use .NNN suffix like ATT&CK.

Note: atlas.mitre.org is a client-side rendered single-page application (SPA). The technique/tactic/study URLs use client-side routing — they work in browsers but return HTTP 404 on direct server requests. This is a known limitation; the URLs are correct for browser navigation.

Relationships to other SecID types: ATLAS techniques map to CWE weaknesses (e.g., AML.T0043 Prompt Injection → CWE-1427) and OWASP LLM Top 10 categories.

children.description: ATLAS sub-technique (e.g., AML.T0043.000 Direct Prompt Injection). Dots preserved in URL.

examples.note: Dots preserved in URL (unlike ATT&CK sub-techniques)

match_nodes.description: Common Attack Pattern Enumeration and Classification

data.official_name: Common Attack Pattern Enumeration and Classification
data.description: Higher-abstraction attack patterns that link to CWE weaknesses.
data.notes:
CAPEC describes attack patterns at a higher abstraction level than ATT&CK techniques. Each CAPEC entry documents: attack prerequisites, required skills, related CWEs (the weakness exploited), mitigations, and examples. Over 550 attack patterns.

CAPEC IDs use the CAPEC- prefix followed by a numeric ID. The URL uses only the numeric portion. Hierarchical — some patterns are children of others (e.g., CAPEC-66 SQL Injection is a child of CAPEC-248 Command Injection).

Relationships: CAPEC patterns map to CWE weaknesses (the flaw being exploited) and to ATT&CK techniques (the adversary behavior).

urls.note: Full CAPEC list in XML format

children.description: CAPEC attack pattern (e.g., CAPEC-66 SQL Injection)

match_nodes.description: FiGHT — 5G threat framework modeled after ATT&CK

data.description: MITRE FiGHT is a framework for understanding threats to 5G networks. Modeled after ATT&CK with tactics, techniques, and sub-techniques specific to 5G telecommunications.
data.notes:
FiGHT extends the ATT&CK model for 5G-specific threats. Includes tactics, techniques, sub-techniques, mitigations, and data sources. Technique IDs use the FGT prefix (e.g., FGT1040 for Network Sniffing, FGT5012 for 5G-specific techniques). IDs starting with FGT1xxx often correspond to ATT&CK techniques adapted for 5G, while FGT5xxx IDs are 5G-specific techniques. Sub-techniques use dot notation (e.g., FGT1040.501).

The FiGHT website (fight.mitre.org) is a client-side rendered single-page application. Technique URLs use client-side routing — they work in browsers but may not resolve on direct server requests.

children.description: FiGHT technique (e.g., FGT1040 Network Sniffing, FGT5012 5G-specific technique)

data.note: FiGHT website is a client-side SPA; URLs work in browsers but may not resolve on direct server requests.

children.description: FiGHT sub-technique (e.g., FGT1592.501). Sub-technique URLs use the full dotted ID.
